topic: Artemis II mission to the Moon
prerequisites: reading distances and times on scientific scales
basic idea of gravity and orbits
difference between altitude and distance from Earth's center

core_definitions.definition: The region of space extending from Earth orbit out to and around the Moon, including the Earth-Moon system used for transfer trajectories.
core_definitions.intuition: It is the neighborhood between Earth and the Moon where Artemis II travels.

core_definitions.definition: A trajectory shaped by the Earth-Moon gravity field so that a spacecraft loops around the Moon and naturally returns toward Earth without needing a large return burn at the Moon.
core_definitions.intuition: It is a built-in safety arc: if the major burn back home were unavailable, gravity still points Orion back toward Earth.

core_definitions.definition: An engine burn used to increase the spacecraft's low point in Earth orbit so the geometry is set correctly for the later translunar injection burn.
core_definitions.intuition: Before heading to the Moon, Orion first reshapes its parking orbit into the right launch lane.

core_definitions.definition: The major outbound burn that places Orion onto its Earth-to-Moon transfer path.
core_definitions.intuition: This is the push that turns an Earth-orbiting spacecraft into a Moon-bound spacecraft.

core_definitions.definition: The region where the Moon's gravity becomes more dominant for the spacecraft's local motion than Earth's gravity.
core_definitions.intuition: This marks the handoff from 'mostly Earth-controlled' motion to 'mostly Moon-guided' motion.

core_definitions.definition: The point of minimum altitude above the lunar surface during the flyby.
core_definitions.intuition: This is the most dramatic point of the mission, when Orion is nearest the Moon and moving fastest around it.

core_definitions.definition: The point where the returning crew module first begins to significantly encounter Earth's atmosphere during reentry.
core_definitions.intuition: This is where the trip home changes from spaceflight to atmospheric flight.

key_theorems.name: Inverse-square gravity as mission-shaping principle
key_theorems.statement: Gravitational attraction decreases approximately as 1/r^2, so the relative influence of Earth and Moon changes along the trajectory and allows a free-return path to be shaped by both bodies.

key_theorems.name: Orbital energy changes through short engine burns
key_theorems.statement: Short propulsion burns change a spacecraft's velocity vector and therefore its orbital energy and path, allowing safe orbit insertion, perigee raising, translunar injection, and trajectory correction.

key_theorems.name: Scale-model proportionality
key_theorems.statement: If one visualization unit represents a fixed physical distance, then all radii, altitudes, and path lengths must be converted by the same scale factor to preserve geometric relationships.

proofs_and_derivations.name: Distance-scale conversion for the Earth-Moon system
proofs_and_derivations.steps_summary: Choose a visualization scale such as 1 unit = 10000 km, then convert Earth radius, Moon radius, Earth-Moon distance, high-Earth orbit size, and lunar flyby altitude by dividing each physical distance by the scale factor.
proofs_and_derivations.visualization_hint: Show the actual distances as labels, then slide between km values and model-unit values.

proofs_and_derivations.name: Why a free-return path comes back without a large return burn
proofs_and_derivations.steps_summary: Explain qualitatively how translunar injection places Orion on a Moon-crossing trajectory, the Moon bends the path on the far side, and the outbound geometry is chosen so Earth's gravity captures the return leg.
proofs_and_derivations.visualization_hint: Animate the same curve in Earth-fixed and Moon-fixed views while highlighting where control shifts from Earth-dominated to Moon-shaped motion.

proofs_and_derivations.name: Mission timeline segmentation
proofs_and_derivations.steps_summary: Break the mission into ascent, checkout orbit, high-Earth orbit, translunar cruise, lunar flyby, trans-Earth return, and entry; map NASA's elapsed times to spatial milestones.
proofs_and_derivations.visualization_hint: Use a mission-phase slider that moves a spacecraft icon through labeled trajectory checkpoints.

worked_examples.description: Earth-Moon scale model for the lesson
worked_examples.visualization_hint: Place Earth at the origin and the Moon near x = 38.44 units, then overlay orbit and flyby markers.

worked_examples.description: High-Earth orbit used before translunar injection
worked_examples.setup: NASA states Orion's safe high Earth orbit is approximately 44525 x 115 statute miles.
worked_examples.visualization_hint: Draw an elongated Earth orbit around the scaled Earth and show how the orbit changes after the apogee raise burn.

worked_examples.setup: NASA's press kit says the exact closest approach depends on launch date and ranges from 4000 to 6000 miles above the lunar surface.
worked_examples.visualization_hint: Provide a slider for flyby altitude within the NASA range and show how the arc curvature changes modestly while the free-return logic stays intact.

worked_examples.setup: Use Orion return values for a deep-space crewed mission.
worked_examples.visualization_hint: Show an entry corridor and a heat bar or color ramp during the final phase scene.

geometric_intuitions.intuition: The Moon is far away relative to the size of Earth, so even a shallow-looking line on the diagram hides a journey of several days.
geometric_intuitions.visualization_suggestion: Let users switch between Earth-close, Moon-close, and full-system views at the same underlying scale.

geometric_intuitions.concept: Why the mission traces a figure-eight-like path
geometric_intuitions.intuition: In an Earth-centered frame, the outbound leg, Moon loop, and return leg together make a stretched figure-eight shape because the Moon bends the path instead of capturing Orion into orbit.
geometric_intuitions.visualization_suggestion: Render the full nominal path with a progress marker and ghost trail.

geometric_intuitions.concept: Burns change the path at a few critical moments
geometric_intuitions.intuition: Most of the mission is coasting; a handful of carefully timed burns do the orbital steering.
geometric_intuitions.visualization_suggestion: Use phase markers and pulse effects only at burn events, not continuously.

geometric_intuitions.concept: Communication blackout near the far side
geometric_intuitions.intuition: The Moon physically blocks the radio line of sight to Earth for tens of minutes when Orion passes behind it.
geometric_intuitions.visualization_suggestion: Draw a line-of-sight segment from Earth to Orion that disappears behind the Moon during closest approach.

real_world_applications.description: Artemis II is the first crewed integrated test of SLS, Orion life support, deep-space communications, and recovery operations for later lunar missions.
real_world_applications.example: The crew practices manual piloting near the ICPS to prepare for future rendezvous and docking tasks.

real_world_applications.description: Free-return trajectories are a classic example of designing with gravity rather than fighting it with propellant.
real_world_applications.example: Artemis II uses the Earth-Moon gravity field so the return path does not require a major burn at the Moon.

real_world_applications.description: Deep-space return requires stronger thermal protection and parachute sequencing than low-Earth-orbit missions.
real_world_applications.example: Orion's 16.5-foot heat shield must manage about 25000 mph reentry speeds and nearly 5000 F exterior temperatures.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Artemis II will land on the Moon.
common_misconceptions.correction: Artemis II is a crewed lunar flyby mission, not a landing mission.
common_misconceptions.teaching_strategy: Contrast Artemis II's free-return loop with a hypothetical lunar-orbit insertion or landing profile.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The spacecraft continuously fires engines all the way to the Moon.
common_misconceptions.correction: Most of the mission is coast flight with only a few major burns and several small trajectory correction burns.
common_misconceptions.teaching_strategy: Add timeline markers for burns and leave the rest of the trajectory visually unpowered.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Closest approach is fixed at a single exact altitude.
common_misconceptions.correction: NASA states the flyby altitude depends on launch date and falls in a range of roughly 4000 to 6000 miles above the lunar surface.
common_misconceptions.teaching_strategy: Use a slider over the allowed range and explain why launch timing changes geometry.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Free return means no navigation or corrections are required.
common_misconceptions.correction: The overall path is free-return, but Orion still performs trajectory correction burns to stay on target and ensure a safe entry setup.
common_misconceptions.teaching_strategy: Show small correction markers on both outbound and inbound legs.

timeline_checkpoints.label: apogee raise burn to high Earth orbit
perigee raise burn for translunar setup

citations.text: NASA. "Artemis II Press Kit." Updated February 10, 2026. https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/
citations.relevance: Primary source for mission overview, high-Earth orbit, trajectory checkpoints, flyby altitude range, blackout duration, and mission priorities.
Current mission summary page for crew size, mission type, and planned duration.
Primary source for SLS Block 1 dimensions, thrust, booster specifications, and core stage parameters.

citations.text: NASA. "SLS Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage." Updated February 10, 2026. https://www.nasa.gov/reference/icps/
citations.relevance: Primary source for ICPS role on Artemis II, stage dimensions, and RL10 thrust.
Primary source for Orion crew capacity, mission duration capability, size, and service-module functions.
Primary source for heat shield size, reentry speed, and thermal loads.

citations.text: NASA Scientific Visualization Studio. "Nominal (reference) Artemis II mission trajectory." Released January 27, 2026. https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/5610
citations.relevance: Reference visualization for the overall Earth-Moon-Earth path and Moon-fixed flyby geometry.

citations.text: NASA Scientific Visualization Studio API. "Nominal (reference) Artemis II mission trajectory" record 5610. Accessed April 2, 2026. https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/api/5610
citations.relevance: Machine-readable source confirming that the displayed trajectory is a nominal reference path, includes Moon-fixed flyby media assets, and may vary with final launch timing.
